Chemistry
The Molecular Nature of Matter and Change Tenth Edition
Martin S. Silberberg and Patricia G. Amateis
© McGraw Hill LLC. All rights reserved. No reproduction or distribution without the prior written consent of McGraw Hill LLC.

Chapter 9: Models of Chemical Bonding
9.1 Atomic Properties and Chemical Bonds.
9.2 The Ionic Bonding Model.
9.3 The Covalent Bonding Model.
9.4 Bond Energy and Chemical Change.
9.5 Between the Extremes: Electronegativity and Bond Polarity.
9.6 An Introduction to Metallic Bonding.

A Comparison of Metals and Nonmetals
Figure 9.1
Access the text alternative for slide images. [slide65.xml]

Types of Chemical Bonding
Ionic bonding involves the transfer of electrons and is usually observed when a metal bonds to a nonmetal.
Covalent bonding involves the sharing of electrons and is usually observed when a nonmetal bonds to a nonmetal.
Metallic bonding involves electron pooling and occurs when a metal bonds to another metal.

Three Models of Chemical Bonding
Figure 9.2

Gradations in Bond Type Among Period 3 and Group 4A Elements
Figure 9.3

Lewis Electron-Dot Symbols
To draw the Lewis symbol for any main-group element:
The group number indicates the number of valence electrons: Group 1 and 2 elements have 1 and 2 valence electrons, respectively, while Groups 1 3 –1 8 elements have 3–8 valence electrons, respectively.
Place one dot at a time on each of the four sides of the element symbol.
Keep adding dots, pairing them, until all are used up.
Example: Nitrogen, N, is in Group 15 and therefore has 5 valence electrons.

Lewis Symbols and Bonding
For a metal, the total number of dots in the Lewis symbol is the number of electrons the atom loses to form a cation.
For a nonmetal, the number of unpaired dots equals
The number of electrons the atom gains to form an anion.
Or the number it shares to form covalent bonds.
The octet rule states that when atoms bond, they lose, gain, or share electrons to attain a filled outer level of 8 electrons (or 2, for H and L i ).

Lewis Dot Symbols for Some Elements
Figure 9.4
Access the text alternative for slide images. [slide66.xml]

The Ionic Bonding Model
An ionic bond is formed when a metal transfers electrons to a nonmetal to form ions, which attract each other to give a solid compound.
The total number of electrons lost by the metal atom(s) equals the total number of electrons gained by the nonmetal atom(s).

Electron Transfer in the Formation of L i ⁺ and F ⁻
Figure 9.5
Access the text alternative for slide images. [slide67.xml]

Sample Problem 9.1: Problem, Plan and Solution
Depicting Ion Formation
PROBLEM: Use partial orbital diagrams and Lewis symbols to depict the formation of
ions from the atoms, and determine the formula of the
compound formed.
PLAN: Draw orbital diagrams and Lewis symbols for N a and O atoms. To attain filled outer levels, N a loses one electron and O gains two. Two N a atoms are needed for each O atom so that the number of electrons lost equals the number of electrons gained.
SOLUTION:
The formula is Na 2 O.
Access the text alternative for slide images. [slide68.xml]

The Exothermic Formation of Sodium Bromide
Figure 9.6
Source: Stephen Frisch/McGraw Hill

The Born-Haber Cycle
Figure 9.7

Lattice Energy
Lattice energy is the energy required to separate 1 mol of an ionic solid into gaseous ions.
Lattice energy is a measure of the strength of the ionic bond.
Coulomb’s law,
Equation 9.1

Effects of Ionic Size and Ionic Charge on Lattice Energy
Figure 9.8

Periodic Trends in Lattice Energy
Lattice energy is affected by ionic size and ionic charge.
As ionic size increases , lattice energy decreases . Lattice energy therefore decreases down a group on the periodic table.
As ionic charge increases , lattice energy increases.

Trends in Lattice Energy
Figure 9.9

Sample Problem 9.2: Problem and Plan
Predicting Relative Lattice Energies from Ionic Properties
PROBLEM: Use ionic properties to explain which compound in each pair has the larger lattice energy: (a) R b I or N a B r; (b) K C l or C a S.
PLAN: To choose the compound with the larger lattice energy, we apply Coulomb’s law and periodic trends in ionic radius and charge. We examine the ions in each compound: for ions of similar size, higher charge leads to a larger lattice energy; for ions with the same charge, smaller size leads to larger lattice energy because the ions can get closer together.

Sample Problem 9.2: Solution
SOLUTION:
(a) NaBr . All the ions have single charges, so charge is not involved. Size increases down a group, so R b ⁺ is larger than N a ⁺ , and I ⁻ is larger than B r ⁻ . Therefore, N a B r has the larger lattice energy because it consists of smaller ions.
(b) CaS . Size decreases from left to right, so K ⁺ is slightly larger than
slightly larger than C I – . However, these small differences
are not nearly as important as the charges:
have twice the
charge of K ⁺ and C l ⁻ , so C a S has the larger lattice energy.

Properties of Ionic Compounds
Ionic compounds tend to be hard, rigid, and brittle, with high melting points.
Ionic compounds do not conduct electricity in the solid state.
In the solid state, the ions are fixed in place in the lattice and do not move.
Ionic compounds conduct electricity when melted or dissolved.
In the liquid state or in solution, the ions are free to move and carry a current.

Why Ionic Compounds Crack
Figure 9.10
Source: (A) Stephen Frisch/McGraw Hill

Electrical Conductivity and Ion Mobility
Figure 9.11
Source: Stephen Frisch/McGraw Hill

Ion Pairs Formed When an Ionic Compound Vaporizes
Interionic attractions are so strong that when an ionic compound is vaporized, ion pairs are formed.
Figure 9.12

Covalent Bond Formation in H 2
Figure 9.13

Distribution of Electron Density in H 2
A, Electron density (blue shading) is high around and between the nuclei. Electron density doubles with each concentric curve. B, The highest regions of electron density are shown as peaks.
Figure 9.14

Bonding Pairs and Lone Pairs
Atoms share electrons to achieve a full outer level of electrons. The shared electrons are called a shared pair or bonding pair .
The shared pair is represented as a pair of dots or a line:
An outer-level electron pair that is not involved in bonding is called a lone pair, or unshared pair .

Properties of a Covalent Bond
The bond order is the number of electron pairs being shared by a given pair of atoms.
A single bond consists of one bonding pair (two electrons) and has a bond order of 1.
A double bond consists of two bonding pairs (four electrons) and has a bond order of 2.
A triple bond consists of three bonding pairs (six electrons) and has a bond order of 3.
The bond energy (B E) is the energy needed to overcome the attraction between the nuclei and the shared electrons. The stronger the bond the higher the bond energy.
The bond length is the distance between the nuclei of the bonded atoms.

Trends in Bond Order, Energy, and Length
For a given pair of atoms, a higher bond order results in a shorter bond length and higher bond energy .
For a given pair of atoms, a shorter bond is a stronger bond.
Bond length increases down a group in the periodic table and decreases across the period.
Bond energy shows the opposite trend.

(b) To compare the single and double bonds involving N, recall that bond strength increases and bond length decreases as bond order increases. To compare the two double bonds, recall that bond length increases and bond strength decreases as atomic radius increases.

Bond Energies and delta H degrees sub rxn
The heat released or absorbed during a chemical change is due to differences between the bond energies of reactants and products.
A certain quantity of heat is absorbed ( Δ H° > 0) to break the reactant bonds and form separate atoms.
A different quantity of heat is released ( Δ H° < 0) when the atoms form product bonds.
Equation 9.2

Figure 9.18

Electronegativity and Bond Polarity
A covalent bond in which the shared electron pair is not shared equally, but remains closer to one atom than the other, is a polar covalent bond.
The ability of an atom in a covalent bond to attract the shared electron pair is called its electronegativity.
Unequal sharing of electrons causes the more electronegative atom of the bond to be partially negative and the less electronegative atom to be partially positive.

The Pauling Electronegativity (E N) Scale
Figure 9.20
Access the text alternative for slide images. [slide71.xml]

Trends in Electronegativity
The most electronegative element is fluorine.
In general electronegativity decreases down a group as atomic size increases.
In general electronegativity increases across a period as atomic size decreases.
Nonmetals tend to be more electronegative than metals.

Electronegativity and Atomic Size
Figure 9.21
Access the text alternative for slide images. [slide72.xml]

Depicting Polar Bonds
The unequal sharing of electrons can be depicted by a polar arrow. The head of the arrow points to the more electronegative element.
A polar bond can also be marked using δ+ and δ− symbols.

Electron Density distributions in H 2 , F 2 , and H F
Figure 9.22
Access the text alternative for slide images. [slide73.xml]

Determining Bond Polarity from E N Values
PROBLEM: (a) Use a polar arrow to indicate the polarity of each bond:
(b) Rank the following bonds in
order of increasing polarity and decreasing percent ionic character:
PLAN: (a) We use Figure 9.20 to find the E N values for the atoms and point the polar arrow toward the more electronegative atom, which is the negative end of the bond. (b) To rank the bond polarity, we determine ΔE N: the higher the value, the greater the polarity. Percent ionic character is also directly related to ΔE N (and bond polarity); it decreases in the opposite order that polarity increases.

Figure 9.25
Access the text alternative for slide images. [slide75.xml]

Properties of the Period 3 Chlorides
Figure 9.26
Source: Stephen Frisch/McGraw-Hill

Metallic Bonding
The electron sea model of metallic bonding proposes that:
All metal atoms in the sample contribute their valence electrons to form a delocalized electron “sea.”
The metal “ions” (nuclei with core electrons) lie in an orderly array within this mobile sea.
All the atoms in the sample share the electrons.
The metal is held together by the attraction between the metal “cations” and the “sea” of valence electrons.

Properties of Metals
Metals are generally solids with moderate to high melting points and much higher boiling points.
Melting points decrease down a group and increase across a period.
Metals can be shaped without breaking.
The electron sea allows the metal ions to slide past each other.
Metals are good conductors of electricity in both the solid and liquid states.
The electron sea is mobile in both phases.
Metals are good conductors of heat.

Electron configurations: An equation shows addition of lithium atom and fluorine atom yielding lithium ion and fluoride ion. Electron configuration for lithium atom reads 1 s 2, 2 s 1. Electron configuration for fluorine atom reads 1 s 2, 2 s 2, 2 p 5. One electron from 2 s orbital of lithium atom moves to 2 p orbital of fluorine atom to yield lithium ion and fluoride ion. Electron configuration for lithium ion reads 1 s 2, which is equal to helium atom, within brackets. Electron configuration for fluoride ion reads 1 s 2, 2 s 2, 2 p 6, which is equal to neon atom, within brackets. Orbital diagrams: Orbital diagram of lithium atom shows one electron indicated by up arrow and one electron indicated by down arrow in 1 s orbital, one electron indicated by up arrow in 2 s orbital, and three empty shells for 2 p orbital. Orbital diagram of fluorine atom shows one electron indicated by up arrow and one electron indicated by down arrow in 1 s orbital, 2 s orbital, and in the first two shells of 2 p orbital, and one electron indicated by up arrow in the third shell of 2 p orbital. The one electron with upward spin in the 2 s orbital of lithium atom moves to the third shell of 2 p orbital of fluorine atom to yield lithium ion and fluoride ion. Orbital diagram of lithium ion shows one electron indicated by up arrow and one electron indicated by down arrow in 1 s orbital, empty shell for 2 s orbital, and three empty shells for 2 p orbital. Orbital diagram of fluoride ion shows one electron indicated by up arrow and one electron indicated by down arrow in 1 s, 2 s, and 2 p orbitals. Lewis electron-dot symbols: Lewis electron-dot symbol for lithium atom shows element symbol, L i , with an electron dot on the left. Lewis electron-dot symbol for fluorine atom shows element symbol, F, with an electron dot on the left and two electron dots at the top, on the right, and at the bottom. The electron from lithium atom moves to fluorine atom to yield lithium ion and fluoride ion. Lithium ion is represented as L i 1 plus ion. Lewis electron-dot symbol for fluoride ion shows F one minus surrounded by two electron dots at the top, on the right, at the bottom, and on the left.

Return to parent-slide containing images. [slide44.xml]
Different types of reactions and change in enthalpies are as follows: Alkane and oxygen molecule react to yield carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen atoms. Enthalpy increases. Bond energy of the reactants, positive sigma B E, equals 783 kilojoules per mole. Alkane and oxygen molecule also react to yield the products, carbon dioxide and water molecule. Enthalpy, delta H sub reaction, of alkane decreases. Carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen atoms react to yield the products, carbon dioxide and water molecule. Bond energy, negative sigma B E, of C double bond O and O single bond H shows decrease in enthalpy. Alcohol and oxygen molecule react to yield the products, carbon dioxide and water molecule. Enthalpy, delta H sub reaction, of alcohol decreases. Alcohol and oxygen molecule also react to yield carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen atoms. Enthalpy increases. Bond energy of the reactants, positive sigma B E, equals 882 kilojoules per mole.
Return to parent-slide containing images. [slide44.xml]

Electron Density distributions in H 2 , F 2 , and H F - Text Alternative
Return to parent-slide containing images. [slide51.xml]
In hydrogen molecule, electron density is equal sharing, nonpolar. Two equal sized peaks appear. Accompanying electron density model shows two hydrogen atoms bonded to each other represented by blue spheres. Each sphere is surrounded by spherical-shaped electron cloud. The clouds are merged together representing the bonding of hydrogen atoms. The merged area is highlighted in red color, and both ends of the cloud are highlighted in blue color. In fluorine molecule, electron density is equal sharing, nonpolar. Two equal sized peaks appear. Accompanying electron density model shows two fluorine atoms bonded to each other represented by green spheres. Each sphere is surrounded by spherical-shaped electron cloud. The clouds are merged together representing the bonding of fluorine atoms. The merged area is highlighted in red color, and both ends of the cloud are highlighted in blue color. In hydrogen fluoride molecule, electron density is unequal sharing, polar. The size of the peak for fluorine atom is higher than that of the hydrogen atom. Polar arrow points toward the peak for fluorine atom. Accompanying electron density model shows hydrogen atom represented by blue sphere and fluorine atom represented by green sphere bonded to each other. Each sphere is surrounded by spherical-shaped electron cloud. The clouds are merged together representing the bonding of hydrogen and fluorine atoms. Electron cloud for hydrogen atom is highlighted in blue color, and the electron cloud for fluorine atom is highlighted in red color.
Return to parent-slide containing images. [slide51.xml]

Electron Density Distributions in Bonds of the Period 3 Chlorides - Text Alternative
Return to parent-slide containing images. [slide56.xml]
Top image: From sodium chloride to aluminum chloride the shared electron density or the bonding region increases. The electronegativity difference between the constituting atoms of the period chlorides is as follows: sodium and chlorine, 2.1; magnesium and chlorine, 1.8; aluminum and chlorine, 1.5.
Bottom image: From silicon tetrachloride to chlorine the shared electron density or the bonding region increases. The electronegativity difference between the constituting atoms of the period chlorides is as follows: silicon and chlorine, 1.2; phosphorus and chlorine, 0.9; sulfur and chlorine, 0.5, and chlorine and chlorine, 0.0.
